metadata.title: Linguistic and Personological Features of the Doka and Martin Grieving Style Continuum
metadata.journal: OMEGA - Journal of Death and Dying

abstract: While there is extensive research on the adaptive grief styles developed by Doka and Martin, this study is the first of its kind to explore the language used among each style of grief. This study used clinical vignettes from a variety of sources on instrumental and intuitive grieving in an attempt to decipher the language use across various linguistic and psychological processes. Following this analysis, latent Dirichlet allocation (LDA) was used fitting a two-topic model to analyze differences between topics while additionally performing a supervised LDA analysis. The strongest data from this study relate to intuitive grief, which found a higher use of presenttense language in comparison to the instrumental grief style. In addition, results found that the language used by intuitive grievers is slightly more distinguishable than that of its instrumental counterpart. Several implications for counseling and research were developed in response to these findings.

paragraphs: How would one describe a person's grieving? Such a description is likely limited to the way they conduct themselves, rather than the words they use. What if we were told that we can better understand a person's grieving style through language? Language allows a person to look beyond the face value of pain to deeply understand how one adapts to loss in the real world. Through the present study the reader will begin to interpret the expression and experiences of others on a continuum of grief styles.

While publication of corpus linguistic studies has been increasing with a few focusing on grief, not one is about understanding the language used by instrumental and intuitive grievers. A current gap in the literature exists between the concepts of instrumental and intuitive grief and a general understanding of grief language. Due to the nature of intuitive and instrumental grief, much of the existing literature focuses on the behaviors of these styles, rather than the language. One hope is that this research disrupts current practice by enhancing the understanding of the adaptive grief style continuum so that counselors can respond uniquely and appropriately to the grief styles of their clients (Doka & Martin, 2010). A Western bias in counseling currently values emotional expressiveness as more therapeutic than cognitive or behavioral responses (Doka & Martin, 2010). However, the adaptive grieving styles are not specific to the Western thought of many practitioners (Doughty & Hoskins, 2011). The results of this study on the linguistic and personological features of instrumental and intuitive grief could aid in challenging these Western-held beliefs, so that that instrumental grief does not continue to be disfranchised among counselors.

In the review of the literature on language and instrumental and intuitive grief, six themes emerged: (a) technical definitions of grief and bereavement, (b) lifespan variation in grief rates, (c) key definitions, (d) nature of intuitive grief, (e) nature of instrumental grief, and (f) the relationship of language and grief. Following the review of the literature, the research questions are outlined.

While grief and bereavement are often used interchangeably in the literature, their technical definitions offer greater granularity. According to Bhatia (2009), grief is defined as "an alteration in mood and affect consisting of sadness appropriate to a real loss" (p. 1). On a felt sense level, grief is broad and refers to other losses such as opportunities and functional abilities (Zisook & Shear, 2009). This definition contrasts with the technical definition of bereavement: "feeling of grief or desolation, especially at the death or loss of a loved one" (Bhatia, 2009, p. 48). On a felt sense level, bereavement is the entire experience in the anticipation, death, and adjustment to life following the death of a loved one (Christ et al., 2003). It is important to note that while the phenomenon of grief is much larger than bereavement, much of the literature focused on grief pertains to bereavement-related grief specifically.

Grief is a response to loss that affects individuals universally and across the lifespan. Children begin experiencing grief at a young age, whether through a death in the family or through a shift in family comfort, such as divorce, financial challenges, or frequent moves (Heath et al., 2008). As individuals age, experiencing loss is increased, mainly through losing significant others (Bonanno et al., 2012). As grief is a widely experienced phenomenon across age groups, it is essential to understand how these losses are experienced individually. Much of the recent research in the U.S. has been focused on interventions to resolve grief, which first requires understanding the unique needs at each developmental level.

In the U.S., the occurrence rate of grief fluctuates across the lifespan. Among all child deaths in the U.S. each year, approximately 83% have a surviving sibling, and one in 20 children lose a parent before graduating high school (Torbic, 2011). According to Balk (2008), 22% to 30% of college students, at any given moment, are in the first year of grieving the death of a family member or friend. As for those in later adulthood, people aged 61 and older were found to experience complicated grief at twice the rate of younger adults (Shear et al., 2013). While these statistics indicate variance in grief occurrence across the lifespan, it is essential to explore the influence that cultural and gender identities have on the grieving experience.

Grief, while experienced universally, varies across cultures in the world, particularly based on how it is outwardly expressed. Within each society exists traditions and rituals that prescribe expected behaviors (Doughty et al., 2011), which indicates the grieving patterns that are accepted by the majority and those that are not. For example, researchers compared grief patterns between China and the U.S. and found that those in China reported higher levels of searching for meaning and avoidance towards thinking about the deceased than those in the U.S. (Pressman & Bonanno, 2007). The more understanding counselors are of the grieving patterns across cultures, the more competent they are in their delivery of clinical interventions.

Societal expectations for the way men and women grieve have been a topic of interest across a variety of platforms and within many fields. Differences among grievers are closely associated with gender role socialization; however, though grieving patterns are influenced by gender, they are not determined by gender (Doka & Martin, 2010). Doka and Martin also made it clear that differences in gender are not fixed, and they change throughout generations and time. Changes across time may be a strong contributing factor for why research on grief continues to be published. The nature of intuitive grief and instrumental grief (Doka & Martin, 2010) places gender aside and accounts for how these patterns of grief are experienced on a continuum.

The first grieving style on the adaptive grief style continuum developed by Doka and Martin (2010) is intuitive grief. This form of grief has been defined as responding to a loss through one's shared inner experiences with those around them, often experiencing their loss deeply, intensely, and through tears (Doka & Martin, 2010). An intuitive grieving style resonates more with emotions and less with cognitions. Doka and Martin have found that intuitive grievers experience their grief in waves and that these grievers often lack energy and motivation. While this form of grief shares traits most associated with women, it is not to say that all females are intuitive grievers.

On the opposite end of the grief style continuum is instrumental grief. Grievers in this form tend to not experience their emotions as intensely as their counterparts and often focus their grief in cognitive and behavioral domains (Doka & Martin, 2010). In the absence of intense emotions, the behavior of instrumental grievers can be described as quietly sitting, pondering, or planning activities following a loss. Doka and Martin described this type of grief as one where little affect is shown, often due to the griever's wish to master or control his or her emotions. Like intuitive grief, instrumental patterns often run parallel to traits associated with masculinity, which cause individuals to incorrectly assign instrumental grief to a male's response to loss.

Many researchers' findings on intuitive grief capture the intensity of emotions associated with this response to loss. Using the Grief Pattern Inventory (GPI) to measure the relationship of intuitive grief on items in the Grief Experience Inventory (GEI), researchers found that intuitive grief significantly correlates with subscales on despair, loss of control, depersonalization, and somatization (Martin & Wang, 2006). The intensity of these emotions is consistent with Doka and Martin's (2010) definition that intuitive grief ranges from shock and disbelief to a sense of losing self-control. Beckett and Dykeman (2015) found that counseling students rated themselves as expecting a higher therapeutic bond with intuitive grievers than their instrumental counterparts. Their study demonstrated the influence that patterns of intuitive grieving have on an interpersonal level. Across studies, intuitive grief has maintained findings that support the intensity of emotions, as have the opposite for those possessing more instrumental styles of grieving.

The researchers on instrumental grief note that despite these patterns appearing unhealthy to traditional norms of grief, instrumental patterns of grief have had a positive impact in the ways one copes with loss. In a study on the collective identity of Nicaraguan mothers during a time of political violence, researchers found that group membership moved these mothers from intuitive to more instrumental styles of grieving (Bayard de Volo, 2006). These women began possessing instrumental grieving behaviors around planning and creating projects that memorialized their fallen children, which changed the ways in which they processed their loss emotionally (Bayard de Volo, 2006). In a study conducted by Lange et al. (2020), researchers found that young widows of the Banso tribe of the Cameroon experienced an increase in instrumental grief responses that were not required before their bereavement, such as problemsolving and the adoption of new roles. Because this grieving style manifests through behavioral and cognitive domains and lacks the outward expression of grief through tears, individuals may be perceived as not grieving or not grieving appropriately. Research on instrumental grieving continues to develop within the field of counseling, specifically on how counselors can respond to the needs of those possessing this style.

The study of grief and language, specifically on social media, has grown over time, largely due to the usefulness of text analysis programs such as Linguistic Inquiry Word Count (LIWC). In one study on the language of grief in comments of deceased MySpace users, researchers found that emotionally distressed comments included more first-person singular pronouns, past tense verbs, prepositions, and conjunctions (Brubaker et al., 2012). In comparison to social media posts before and after someone's passing, it was found that postdeath posts revealed more language around social orientation and emotionally positive narratives (Getty et al., 2011). Complicated grievers were found to present with greater negative and anger emotion words than their asymptomatic counterparts (Diminich & Bonanno, 2014). These studies demonstrate the power of language as it specifically relates to the process of grieving. Few studies exist that focus specifically on text analysis with instrumental and intuitive grief.

Given the aforementioned gaps and needs, four research questions were designed to guide this study. The first question was: for instrumental grievers, what is the level of use of linguistic processes known to be related to grief? The second question was: for intuitive grievers, what is the level of use of linguistic processes known to be related to grief? The third research question was: what constellations of linguistic processes best differentiate intuitive from instrumental grievers? The fourth research question was: what linguistic properties best characterize a two-topic model?

paragraphs: The study employed a corpus linguistic design (Weisser, 2016). The variables were: (a) 9 linguistic and emotional processes (continuous), and (b) grief style (binomial). The unit of analysis was tokens (individual words).

sections.paper_id: Geisler and Dykeman - 2023 - Linguistic and Personological Features of the Doka and Martin Grieving Style Continuum
paragraphs: Register, Scope, and Sources. The register for this study was spoken English. The subregister was psychotherapy narratives (analogue and actual) related to instrumental and intuitive grieving. The scope was publicly available clinical vignettes focused on instrumental and intuitive grieving. The sources were clinical vignettes of instrumental and intuitive grieving that were published in Grieving Beyond Gender: Understanding the Ways Men and Women Mourn (Doka & Martin, 2010). Additionally, vignettes were gathered from the work of Beckett and Dykeman (2015) and Doughty and Hoskins (2011).

Preprocessing. Doka and Martin's (2010) book was first used to gather the clinical vignettes for instrumental and intuitive grieving. These vignettes were often identified by the authors immediately prior to, or following, the case example. These cases were then compiled into a Word document. A total of three documents were developed, which included: corpora of both instrumental and intuitive texts, corpora of instrumental texts, and corpora of intuitive texts. All files were then saved as ".txt" documents for use with linguistic software.

Vignettes were stored in two text files according to their instrumental or intuitive classification, with each vignette separated by a newline character ("\n"). To prepare the data for analysis, vignettes were extracted from their respective files and cleaned by stripping whitespace, translating characters to lowercase, removing punctuation, removing stop words, and removing numbers. Finally, a document-term matrix was generated using only word stems. All data processing was accomplished using the R package tm.

In total, 68 vignettes were processed-43 instrumental and 25 intuitive. Before word stemming, the instrumental and intuitive corpora were composed of 2,932 words and 1,686 words, respectively. The final document-term matrix comprised of 68 documents (i.e., vignettes), and 1,237 distinct word stems with a sparsity index of 96%. Word count was small relative to the number of documents, so all word stems were included in the document-term matrix.

sections.paper_id: Geisler and Dykeman - 2023 - Linguistic and Personological Features of the Doka and Martin Grieving Style Continuum
paragraphs: The internal reliability of LIWC was measured by selecting a particular category of words (e.g., anger) and determining the degree of its use across subcorpuses by calculating the mutual relationships of the word use (Pennebaker et al., 2015). Words then became measured as a percentage of all words before becoming an item in the Cronbach alpha calculation (Pennebaker et al., 2015). From here, raw scores for each corpus were produced and corrected, if needed. As for external validity, this can be measured in several ways, including judged, self-reported, and LIWC numbers to reflect a particular word category (Pennebaker et al., 2015). Linguistic Processes. A total of two subcategories under linguistic dimensions were chosen for this study: articles and prepositions. Articles and prepositions are two categories of function words. Articles consist of only three words: "a," "an," and "the." According to Pennebaker (2011), those who used more article words in their narratives tended to be more emotionally stable. Moreover, the use of articles informs how people think, feel, and connect with others in their worlds (Pennebaker, 2011). Examples of prepositions include "to," "with," and "above." According to Pennebaker (2011), prepositions are "glorious language markers" that "help to situate an idea in time and space" (p. 296). Furthermore, Brubaker et al. (2012) found preposition use to be high among emotional distress language.

Psychological Processes. A total of seven subcategories were chosen as they related to the language used when experiencing loss. The selection of these LIWC subcategories was driven by the literature covered in the introduction section. These subcategories were: (a) negative emotion, (b) anger, (c) sadness, (d) insight, (e) past focus, (f) present focus, and (g) future focus. Negative emotion, anger, and sadness were all subcategories of affective processes. Examples of negative emotion words are "hurt," "ugly," and "nasty" and are associated with negative events and loss. Anger has been associated with emotional distress language in grievers (Brubaker et al., 2012) and includes words such as "hate" and "annoyed." Sadness, a commonly associated emotion among grievers, includes words such as "crying" and "grieve." Insight, one of the subcategories of cognitive processes, includes words such as "think," "know," and "consider." Past focus and present focus are two subcategories under time orientation. Past language includes "ago," "did," and "talked," while present focus language includes "today," "is," and "now." Future focus language includes "may," "will," and "soon."

paragraphs: LIWC is a software application "designed to accept written or transcribed verbal text which has been stored as a digital, machine-readable file" (Pennebaker et al., 2015, p. 2). This application includes an internal dictionary containing thousands of words, which are assigned in groups to various domains or categories. When the text words, called target words (Pennebaker et al., 2015), are inputted through the application, LIWC identifies the frequency of these words in their given domain, which provides users with a summary of words per category.

sections.paper_id: Geisler and Dykeman - 2023 - Linguistic and Personological Features of the Doka and Martin Grieving Style Continuum
paragraphs: Research Questions 1-3. For the first two research questions, the descriptive statistics of mean and standard deviation were calculated and reported. For the third research question, the log-likelihood ratio test was used to compare word usage between instrumental and intuitive grievers to detect significant differences in word frequency usage. To measure the effect size of word usage between instrumental and intuitive grievers, we used the Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC) score.

Research Question 4. For the fourth research question, three stages took place. First, we used a latent Dirichlet allocation (LDA) for fitting a two-topic model. Second, we extracted the per-topic-per-word probabilities to analyze the differences within and between the two topics. Third, we evaluated the performance of the LDA classifier using the following metrics: accuracy, specificity, and sensitivity.

LDA Model Specifications.. LDA model with k ¼ 2 topics was fit to the document-term matrix using the variational expectation-maximization (VEM) method as implemented by Blei et al. (2003). The model was fit using the LDA() function from the R package topicmodels.

LDA Model Evaluation.. The fitted LDA model estimated term distributions over topics (b) and topic distributions over documents (c). The first phase of the analysis used the estimated beta scores to identify prominent semantic characteristics of each topic. The second phase of the analysis used the estimated gamma scores to identify the dominant topic within each document and compare predicted topics against professional classifications. The degree to which topic labels correspond to professional labels are measured via McNemar's Chi-squared test with continuity correction. On the condition of a significant chi-square test, topics were mapped onto intuitive or instrumental labels, and the predictive accuracy of the LDA model classifier was tested. Model evaluation tests were conducted using the confusion Matrix() function from the R package caret.

sections.paper_id: Geisler and Dykeman - 2023 - Linguistic and Personological Features of the Doka and Martin Grieving Style Continuum
paragraphs: In regard to the first and second research question, the raw and normal count of linguistic and psychological processes for instrumental and intuitive grievers can be found on Table 1. For the third research question, the constellations of linguistic processes that differentiate instrumental from intuitive grief can also be found on Table 1. The linguistic process category that differentiated intuitive grief language from instrumental was present-focused (LL¼ 40.06, p < .001, BIC 30.90). The linguistic process category that differentiated instrumental grief language from intuitive grief language was past-focused (LL¼ 8.14, p < .05, BIC -1.01).

In terms of the fourth research question, we inspected the top 30 words from each topic as measured by b. Words with relatively large beta scores within a topic were more likely to be associated with that topic as compared to other words in the vocabulary. The words presented in Figure 1 were the most likely words to be associated with topic 1 and topic 2. These were the words that best characterizes their respective topics.

The top word stems in topic 2 tend to have larger beta values than the top word stems in topic 1. For example, the top 30 terms in topic 2 had a cumulative probability of 0.2860, whereas the top 30 terms in topic 1 had a cumulative probability of 0.0729. For reference, the null model (where all terms are assigned equal probability of 1/1237 ¼ 0.0008) had a cumulative 30-word probability of 0.0243. Thus, we concluded that the top terms in topic 2 more strongly characterized topic 2 than the top terms in topic 1.

With the caveat that topic 1 was challenging to characterize due to the flatter distribution of beta values, we could still draw general themes from the top 30 words in each topic. Most notably, we found that words that characterize topic 1 seemed to be object-oriented and words that characterized topic 2 seemed to be subject-oriented. Nouns that describe physical, concrete objects such as "friend," "group," "wife," and "counselor," suggest that topic 1 authors orient their experience in the tangible space shared by external observers. Verbs that describe abstract feelings and sentiments such as "like," "know," "want," "think," "get," and "need," suggest that topic 2 authors are more introspective and leverage their feelings to drive their actions.

We also found that several death-related words ("death," "funer*," "loss," and "die") and time-related words ("later," "year," "month," and "week") had relatively large beta scores in topic 1 but not topic 2. Terms "feel," "talk," and "time" were found in the top 30 lists for both topics. Overall, there is vague indication that topic 1 captured an instrumental grieving style and topic 2 captured an intuitive grieving style, but these interpretations are highly subjective.

Next, we evaluated model performance by identifying the topic that each vignette is most likely to represent and studying the relationship between modeled labels ("topic 1" and "topic 2") and professional labels (instrumental and intuitive). Labels from the LDA model are generated by identifying the topic that corresponds to the maximum gamma value.

On the surface, intuitive vignettes seem to be loosely associated with topic 2, whereas instrumental vignettes have no clear leaning towards one topic or another. For example, a moderate majority (17/25 ¼ 68%) of intuitive vignettes were labeled "topic 2," but only (22/43 ¼ 51%) of instrumental vignettes were labeled "topic 1."

McNemar's chi-squared test with continuity correction revealed statistically significant evidence of an association between modeled labels and professional labels at the alpha ¼ 0.05 significance level: v2 ¼4.9655 (df ¼ 1), p ¼ 0.026. That is, we rejected the null hypothesis of marginal homogeneity.

Next, we tested the accuracy of the LDA classifier by assuming that topic 2 is a proxy for the "intuitive" label, and topic 1 is a proxy for the "instrumental" label. We made these assumptions based on the results of the chi-square test as well as our qualitative observations in the previous section that characterized topic 2 as subject-oriented and topic 1 as object-oriented. The accuracy score for the LDA classifier was 57%, which is low relative to the baseline "no information rate" of 63%. A significance test revealed that the LDA model was no better than a baseline model at matching professional labels with their presumed latent topics at the alpha ¼ 0.05 significance level (p ¼ 0.8706).

The linguistic process category that differentiated instrumental grief language from intuitive grief language was past-focused (LL¼ 8.14, p < .05, BIC -1.01).

In terms of the fourth research question, we inspected the top 30 words from each topic as measured by b. Words with relatively large beta scores within a topic were more likely to be associated with that topic as compared to other words in the vocabulary. The words presented in Figure 1 were the most likely words to be associated with topic 1 and topic 2. These were the words that best characterizes their respective topics.

The top word stems in topic 2 tend to have larger beta values than the top word stems in topic 1. For example, the top 30 terms in topic 2 had a cumulative probability of 0.2860, whereas the top 30 terms in topic 1 had a cumulative probability of 0.0729. For reference, the null model (where all terms are assigned equal probability of 1/1237 ¼ 0.0008) had a cumulative 30-word probability of 0.0243. Thus, we concluded that the top terms in topic 2 more strongly characterized topic 2 than the top terms in topic 1.

Most notably, we found that words that characterize topic 1 seemed to be object-oriented and words that characterized topic 2 seemed to be subject-oriented. Nouns that describe physical, concrete objects such as "friend," "group," "wife," and "counselor," suggest that topic 1 authors orient their experience in the tangible space shared by external observers. Verbs that describe abstract feelings and sentiments such as "like," "know," "want," "think," "get," and "need," suggest that topic 2 authors are more introspective and leverage their feelings to drive their actions.

Terms "feel," "talk," and "time" were found in the top 30 lists for both topics. Overall, there is vague indication that topic 1 captured an instrumental grieving style and topic 2 captured an intuitive grieving style, but these interpretations are highly subjective.

Next, we evaluated model performance by identifying the topic that each vignette is most likely to represent and studying the relationship between modeled labels ("topic 1" and "topic 2") and professional labels (instrumental and intuitive). Labels from the LDA model are generated by identifying the topic that corresponds to the maximum gamma value.

On the surface, intuitive vignettes seem to be loosely associated with topic 2, whereas instrumental vignettes have no clear leaning towards one topic or another. For example, a moderate majority (17/25 ¼ 68%) of intuitive vignettes were labeled "topic 2," but only (22/43 ¼ 51%) of instrumental vignettes were labeled "topic 1."

McNemar's chi-squared test with continuity correction revealed statistically significant evidence of an association between modeled labels and professional labels at the alpha ¼ 0.05 significance level: v2 ¼4.9655 (df ¼ 1), p ¼ 0.026. Next, we tested the accuracy of the LDA classifier by assuming that topic 2 is a proxy for the "intuitive" label, and topic 1 is a proxy for the "instrumental" label. We made these assumptions based on the results of the chi-square test as well as our qualitative observations in the previous section that characterized topic 2 as subject-oriented and topic 1 as object-oriented. The accuracy score for the LDA classifier was 57%, which is low relative to the baseline "no information rate" of 63%. A significance test revealed that the LDA model was no better than a baseline model at matching professional labels with their presumed latent topics at the alpha ¼ 0.05 significance level (p ¼ 0.8706).

sections.paper_id: Geisler and Dykeman - 2023 - Linguistic and Personological Features of the Doka and Martin Grieving Style Continuum
paragraphs: The aim of this study was to explore the linguistic and psychological processes in counseling vignettes of instrumental and intuitive grievers. The two categories of linguistic processes included articles and prepositions. The psychological processes categories included negative emotion, anger, sadness, insight, and past-, present-, and future-focused language. The first research question explored the usage of linguistic and psychological processes for instrumental grievers. The second research question explored these same categories as they pertained to intuitive grievers. For the third question, the constellations of linguistic processes that best differentiated intuitive from instrumental grievers were explored. Using the results from the third research question, the fourth research question explored the predictive strength of the constellations of linguistic processes for each grief style.

The first research question looked at the level of use of linguistic processes known to be related to grief for instrumental grievers while the second research question looked at the linguistic processes for intuitive grievers. In terms of the probable explanations for the obtained results for research questions one and two, they can be found in the discussion for research question three.

In terms of the third research question (instrumental and intuitive griever differences), there are two potential explanations for the obtained results. The first explanation is that instrumental grievers, whose grief patterns are cognitively oriented, focus on the evaluation of the relationship, including both the death and the meaning. The nature of evaluation assumes an event has occurred prior to this cognitive assessment, thus the reason for finding past tense language among this grief style. This explanation is congruent with Doka's theory on the topic (personal communication, July 18, 2020). The second explanation is that instrumental grievers are reflective of their grief experiences, hence the use of past tense. Additional support from coauthor Martin (personal communication, July 18, 2020) was contributed to formulate this explanation. Between the former and the latter, the former is the most probable because instrumental grievers are contemplative (Doka & Martin, 2010), which suggests that there is a level of depth likely focused on past experiences and events with the deceased, similar loss, or the relationship itself. The former explanation suggesting an evaluative aspect mirrors the depth of contemplation, rather than mere reflection.

As for the additional results of the third research question pertaining to the constellations of linguistic processes that differentiate intuitive from instrumental grief, there are two potential explanations that exist for the obtained results. The first explanation, initiated by Doka, is that intuitive grievers tend to focus on processing their current emotions (personal communication, July 18, 2020). In processing current emotions, it is likely that there is more here-and-now language underlying the present-tense findings. An alternative explanation is that intuitive grievers share their emotions in their current environment(s). This theory from Martin (personal communication, July 18, 2020) supports the literature, which states that intuitive grievers are unable or unwilling to distance themselves from emotional expression with others (Doka & Martin, 2010), prompting a level of immediacy that holds value to the present. Between the two explanations, the combination of both makes for a reasonable explanation due to the nature of intuitive grief. The intensity of emotions among this grief style is overall likely eliciting language that is anchored in the griever's here-andnow thoughts, emotions, and experiences.

As for the fourth research question, several potential explanations can be made for the observed results. First, it may be harder to linguistically classify an instrumental griever since these grievers are defined by an absence of both behaviors and expression. Support for this theory can be found in the literature on these grief styles, which states that that instrumental grievers do not possess the level of depth and intensity of feelings that their intuitive counterparts do (Doka & Martin, 2010), and because of what is lacking, it may contribute to the lack of consistent language used to describe their grieving style.

Second, because 68% of intuitive vignettes were labeled as topic 2, and intuitive vignettes are easier to classify than instrumental, it is important to consider what topic 2 possesses and lacks in comparison to topic 1. One possible explanation for why words associated with time ("later," "year," "month," and "week") are in topic 1 but not 2 may have to do with how intuitive grievers use adaptive strategies of centering, or engaging in activities focused on grief itself. According to Doka and Martin (2010), "going with the experience" is a primary adaptive strategy for intuitive grievers in which they "structure their actions by responding to their feelings" (p. 61). Ways in which this is done often involve making space to cry or sharing experiences with others facing loss. This primary adaptive strategy of "going with" and centering may cause the healing of an intuitive griever to be dependent on their feelings rather than measured by the notion of time.

Third, words associated with death ("death," "funer*," "loss," and "die") were found in topic 1 but not topic 2. One possible explanation is that intuitive grievers may avoid talking about the factual events surrounding death and, in turn, are focusing intently on their inner experiences. Nothing could be found related to this interesting phenomenon in the research. Applying Occam's razor, a theory stating that often the simplest explanation is the correct one, suggests that the reason for this may be due to the fact that language around the specifics of loss could heighten the preexisting intensity of grief reactions among this style. Among the three possible explanations, the first and second seem to be the most likely explanations for the obtained results.

Overall, we found a weak association between topics and grief styles and derived little utility in mapping topics onto grief styles for the purposes of classification. At a high level, we take these results to mean that the fitted two-topic LDA model is capturing variation in word usage that is shared by instrumental and intuitive grievers, alike, and therefore cannot be used for differentiating grief styles. If there exists latent topics that map neatly onto grief style, these topics may have been overshadowed by larger variations in the data and require a more complex topic model to be detected.

There were three limitations to the present study that should be considered. The most significant one is sample size. With larger samples, we can fine tune our vocabulary and experiment with a greater number of topics. While-in theory-grief styles can be characterized by distinct semantic patterns, teasing these patterns out of a bag-of-words likely requires a much larger corpus. Relatedly, we are limited by our inability to generalize our findings to new datasets. We made no attempt at fitting a model that was robust to random variation because holding out data for the sake of robustness would have compromised our ability to detect any meaningful trends in the full dataset. Again, larger samples would have mitigated this issue. A second limitation is that vignette labels (and their corresponding latent topics) are not practically independent. Grieving style is manifested on a spectrum and vignette labels represent the most likely grieving style-although they do not preclude other, less prominent grieving styles. An LDA model assumes topics to be independent of each other, and we may have been better off fitting a more sophisticated model that relaxes this assumption of independence. The third limitation is that distinction from analog and actual narratives from instrumental and intuitive grievers is unknown. Thus, it is possible that language in the vignettes may not truly capture the language used among each of the grief styles.

There are four implications for counseling practice that can be drawn from the obtained results. First, as it relates to intuitive grief, clinicians who are keenly aware of their clients using more present-focused language may be inclined to use interventions more anchored in the here-and-now, to match the need of the intuitive griever. Furthermore, written homework assignments related to grief that clinicians suggest, such as journaling, or writing a eulogy, a letter to the deceased, memoirs, or poems, can be explored with clients and act as windows into how present-focused language relates to their processing and reactions surrounding the loss.

A second practice implication is that because words associated with time may not be favorable to the intuitive griever, counselors should be sensitive to not use interventions or set goals that have implicit messages surrounding a timeline for when grief reactions should subside. Similarly, as intuitive grievers may not be focused on language surrounding the loss itself, interventions that focus on emotional awareness and expression should be favored over those that focus on the granularity of details surrounding one's death.

A third practice implication is that while the results related to instrumental grievers are less pronounced, counselors need to be cognizant of the clients' use of past tense language and grief reactions that match an instrumental griever. At this time, it is unknown if the past tense language relates to the actual death or life of a loved one. However, one could assume that work around the meaning of the relationship would complement the evaluative nature of instrumental grief. Therefore, choosing reflective interventions or assignments for the client to work on in and out of session may have more therapeutic benefit than those that have implications for the future and moving forward. In essence, sometimes looking backwards is the path to moving forward for the instrumental griever.

The final implication is that counselors should be mindful that while grief styles are individualized and vary from one person to the next, there exists cultural and systemic influences at various degrees. Many aspects of our culture, for example, still operate on a binary gender identity framework around grief. It is recommended that counselors conceptualize cases related to grief from a socioecological model and build awareness for how these influences exist in the therapy room. The clients' social locations that include race, ethnicity, regionality, and sexual orientation are just a few identities that should be explored as they relate to their intersection of culturally expected versus experienced grief response. Counselors who grasp an understanding of the influences of systems may be at a greater advantage in preventing language that perpetuates stereotypes surrounding grief.

Two implications for research should be noted. First, because the results demonstrate that the language between grieving styles is to some degrees distinguishable, researchers have new opportunities to further explore language, specifically. It may be interesting for researchers to study participants' individual grief styles based on their results of the Grief Pattern Inventory (GPI; Doka & Martin, 2010) and their associated language use in journals, eulogies, or transcribed therapy sessions focused on loss. Second, expanding on the results of this study would also allow researchers to study online blogs focused on grief to determine the type of griever that utilizes web-based social groups as a means of coping with loss. Moreover, corpus linguistic studies using memoirs on grief and loss can be used to identify grief styles. Among the various avenues for future research that may come as a result of this study, those that highlight instrumental grief specifically, may aid the field of counseling in recognizing the existence of the instrumental grief style in addition to the highly normalized/enfranchised intuitive grief style in U.S. culture.

The final implication is that counselors should be mindful that while grief styles are individualized and vary from one person to the next, there exists cultural and systemic influences at various degrees. Many aspects of our culture, for example, still operate on a binary gender identity framework around grief. It is recommended that counselors conceptualize cases related to grief from a socioecological model and build awareness for how these influences exist in the therapy room. The clients' social locations that include race, ethnicity, regionality, and sexual orientation are just a few identities that should be explored as they relate to their intersection of culturally expected versus experienced grief response. Counselors who grasp an understanding of the influences of systems may be at a greater advantage in preventing language that perpetuates stereotypes surrounding grief.

Two implications for research should be noted. First, because the results demonstrate that the language between grieving styles is to some degrees distinguishable, researchers have new opportunities to further explore language, specifically. It may be interesting for researchers to study participants' individual grief styles based on their results of the Grief Pattern Inventory (GPI; Doka & Martin, 2010) and their associated language use in journals, eulogies, or transcribed therapy sessions focused on loss. Second, expanding on the results of this study would also allow researchers to study online blogs focused on grief to determine the type of griever that utilizes web-based social groups as a means of coping with loss. Moreover, corpus linguistic studies using memoirs on grief and loss can be used to identify grief styles. Among the various avenues for future research that may come as a result of this study, those that highlight instrumental grief specifically, may aid the field of counseling in recognizing the existence of the instrumental grief style in addition to the highly normalized/enfranchised intuitive grief style in U.S. culture.
sections.preview_head: The aim of this study was to explore the linguistic and psychological processes in counseling vignettes of instrumental and intuitive grievers. The two categories of linguistic processes included articles and prepositions. The psychological processes categories included negative emotion, anger, sadness, insight, and past-, present-, and future-focused language.
